edit_diagnosis: Dense shaping components r_approach_region, r_stability, and r_landing_quality are too generous, allowing the agent to exploit repeated contact toggling and collect high reward without ever completing a stable landing. This is a reward hack, not a false positive. The fix requires simultaneously reducing those shaping weights and increasing the one‑time stable‑landing bonus to shift the policy gradient toward successful, fast landings.

The reward schema still over-emphasizes dense shaping, resulting in only 30% landing success and persistent unstable contacts. Further coupled rebalancing—decreasing dense weights and significantly boosting the one‑time stable‑landing bonus, with an optional progress boost—is the safe, proven path to shift the policy toward reliable landings.

Agent lands with 70% success but dense shaping encourages prolonged bouncing; further coupled rebalancing of dense weights (halving r_stability and r_landing_quality) and terminal bonus (20% boost) with progress‑based shaping increase (50% boost on r_approach_progress) should improve speed and task performance while retaining adequate guidance.

Agent achieves perfect landings but takes ~940 steps per episode because per‑step dense rewards create a marginal gain for lingering. The lack of any time penalty or speed incentive is the root cause. No reward hack; only task failure. Memory warns that further dense weight reduction without a speed lever will worsen control. Since the required step‑penalty operator is not available, a structural search for a time‑aware component is indicated.
